result.preview:
[{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. Been looking for something like this for a long time.", "text": "Looked online for years for something like this. It's the best I've seen.", "review_time": "2020-08-12 11:06:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_8"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Referance Guide", "text": "Good reference guide for the basics", "review_time": "2014-11-13 18:55:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_178"}]

args.code:
import json
import re

# Load the books data from the file
books_file = var_functions.query_db:8
books_data = []
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    for line in f:
        # Parse each JSON object
        try:
            # The file contains JSON objects, one per line or as a JSON array
            books_data = json.load(f)
            break
        except:
            pass

# If that didn't work, try reading as list of dicts
if not books_data:
    with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
        content = f.read()
        try:
            books_data = json.loads(content)
        except:
            pass

print(f"Number of books loaded: {len(books_data)}")

# Look at the structure of details to extract publication year
sample_details = [book.get('details', '') for book in books_data[:10]]
print("Sample details:")
for i, detail in enumerate(sample_details, 1):
    print(f"Book {i}: {detail[:200]}...")
    
# Try to extract years from details
year_pattern = r'(\d{4})'
decades = []
books_with_years = []

for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    book_id = book.get('book_id', '')
    rating_number = book.get('rating_number', '0')
    
    # Find all 4-digit numbers that could be years (1900-2023)
    years = re.findall(year_pattern, details)
    valid_years = [int(year) for year in years if 1900 <= int(year) <= 2023]
    
    if valid_years:
        # Take the earliest year as publication year (most details have "first edition" or publication date)
        pub_year = min(valid_years)
        decade = (pub_year // 10) * 10
        decades.append(decade)
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book_id,
            'pub_year': pub_year,
            'decade': decade,
            'rating_number': int(rating_number) if rating_number else 0
        })

print(f"\nBooks with extractable years: {len(books_with_years)}")
print(f"Unique decades found: {sorted(set(decades))}")

# Save results for next steps
print("\n__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(books_with_years[:20]))  # Return first 20 as sample

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re

# Access the file path from the storage
books_file = var_functions.query_db:8
print(f"Books data file: {books_file}")
# Read the full data
books_data = []
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

print(f"Number of books loaded: {len(books_data)}")

# Function to extract publication year from details text
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text:
        return None
    
    # Look for publication patterns
    patterns = [
        r'published.*?\b(\d{4})\b',
        r'edition on.*?\b(\d{4})\b', 
        r'released on.*?\b(\d{4})\b',
        r'first edition.*?\b(\d{4})\b',
        r'published by.*?\b(\d{4})\b',
        r'\b(19\d{2}|20[01]\d|202[0-3])\b'  # 1900-2023
    ]
    
    years = []
    for pattern in patterns:
        matches = re.findall(pattern, details_text, re.IGNORECASE)
        years.extend([int(m) for m in matches if 1900 <= int(m) <= 2023])
    
    return min(years) if years else None

# Extract publication years
books_with_decades = []
for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    pub_year = extract_year(details)
    
    if pub_year:
        decade = (pub_year // 10) * 10
        rating_number = book.get('rating_number', '0')
        
        try:
            rating_count = int(rating_number) if rating_number else 0
        except:
            rating_count = 0
            
        books_with_decades.append({
            'book_id': book.get('book_id'),
            'pub_year': pub_year,
            'decade': decade,
            'rating_count': rating_count
        })

print(f"Books with publication year: {len(books_with_decades)}")
print(f"Decades range: {min([b['decade'] for b in books_with_decades])} to {max([b['decade'] for b in books_with_decades])}")

# Show some examples
for i, book in enumerate(books_with_decades[:10]):
    print(f"{i+1}. {book['book_id']} - Year: {book['pub_year']}, Decade: {book['decade']}, Ratings: {book['rating_count']}")

# Return data for analysis
result = {
    'books_with_decades': books_with_decades,
    'summary': {
        'total_books': len(books_data),
        'books_with_year': len(books_with_decades),
        'unique_decades': len(set(b['decade'] for b in books_with_decades))
    }
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re

# Access the file path from the storage
books_file = var_functions.query_db:8
print(f"Books data file: {books_file}")
# Read the full data
books_data = []
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

print(f"Number of books loaded: {len(books_data)}")

# Function to extract publication year from details text
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text:
        return None
    
    # Look for publication patterns
    patterns = [
        r'published.*?\b(\d{4})\b',
        r'edition on.*?\b(\d{4})\b', 
        r'released on.*?\b(\d{4})\b',
        r'first edition.*?\b(\d{4})\b',
        r'published by.*?\b(\d{4})\b',
        r'\b(19\d{2}|20[01]\d|202[0-3])\b'  # 1900-2023
    ]
    
    years = []
    for pattern in patterns:
        matches = re.findall(pattern, details_text, re.IGNORECASE)
        years.extend([int(m) for m in matches if 1900 <= int(m) <= 2023])
    
    return min(years) if years else None

# Extract publication years
books_with_decades = []
for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    pub_year = extract_year(details)
    
    if pub_year:
        decade = (pub_year // 10) * 10
        rating_number = book.get('rating_number', '0')
        
        try:
            rating_count = int(rating_number) if rating_number else 0
        except:
            rating_count = 0
            
        books_with_decades.append({
            'book_id': book.get('book_id'),
            'pub_year': pub_year,
            'decade': decade,
            'rating_count': rating_count
        })

print(f"Books with publication year: {len(books_with_decades)}")
print(f"Decades range: {min([b['decade'] for b in books_with_decades])} to {max([b['decade'] for b in books_with_decades])}")

# Show some examples
for i, book in enumerate(books_with_decades[:10]):
    print(f"{i+1}. {book['book_id']} - Year: {book['pub_year']}, Decade: {book['decade']}, Ratings: {book['rating_count']}")

# Return data for analysis
result = {
    'books_with_decades': books_with_decades,
    'summary': {
        'total_books': len(books_data),
        'books_with_year': len(books_with_decades),
        'unique_decades': len(set(b['decade'] for b in books_with_decades))
    }
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# The storage key contains the file path
books_file_path = var_functions.query_db:8

# Read the data
with open(books_file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

print(f"Loaded {len(books_data)} books")

# Function to extract year
def extract_year(details):
    if not details:
        return None
    
    # Find 4-digit numbers between 1900-2023
    years = re.findall(r'(\b(19\d{2}|20[01]\d|202[0-3])\b)', details)
    
    if years:
        # Get the earliest year (publication year, not later edition years)
        year_ints = [int(year[0]) for year in years]
        # Filter for reasonable publication years
        valid_years = [y for y in year_ints if 1900 <= y <= 2023]
        if valid_years:
            return min(valid_years)
    return None

# Process books
processed_books = []
for book in books_data:
    pub_year = extract_year(book.get('details', ''))
    if pub_year:
        decade = (pub_year // 10) * 10
        try:
            rating_count = int(book.get('rating_number', 0))
        except:
            rating_count = 0
            
        processed_books.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'pub_year': pub_year,
            'decade': decade,
            'rating_count': rating_count
        })

print(f"Found publication year for {len(processed_books)} books")

# Show decade distribution
decade_counts = {}
for book in processed_books:
    decade = book['decade']
    decade_counts[decade] = decade_counts.get(decade, 0) + 1

print(f"Decades found: {sorted(decade_counts.items())}")

# Return processed data
result = json.dumps(processed_books)
print("__RESULT__:")
print(result)

I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

Although we've been here for 20 years, I would say that it's only been within the past five years that this former city girl has embraced the country lifestyle. That lifestyle includes hunting, something that I've almost lost some city friends over.

My feelings about hunting are as follows...although I myself eat very little meat, my dogs are my family, and what can I say, it's a family of carnivores! In addition, when you are aware of the dark side of those pretty plastic wrapped packages of meat in the supermarket, hunting seems pretty darn humane, and a reasonable way to procure meat for your family. I think of what the cows and chickens made into dog kibble went through, and what diseases they might have had before being in a can of cat food.

That being said, I'm always interested in different perspectives about hunting, lifestyles, and philosophies.

I enjoyed this book quite a bit because it contains all of the above. There seem to be so many books about hunting, magazines about hunting. Books on bow hunting, and dialogue about hunting a mule deer as opposed to a white tailed deer. I felt like this book fills a gap that had not been covered in any of the articles or books I had previously read.

Because I am passionate about knowing where my food comes from, and doing even a small part to make choices that are responsible, it was refreshing and affirming to read the author's perspective. When we grab a fast food hamburger, we don't think about the suffering animal that probably led an unnatural life, and we certainly don't think of the workers in the processing plants that do a job so brutal and often dangerous. WITH book_years AS (
  SELECT 
    book_id,
    CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '(19[0-9]{2}|20[0-2][0-3])') AS INTEGER) as pub_year,
    rating_number
  FROM books_info
  WHERE details ~ '(19[0-9]{2}|20[0-2][0-3])'
),
book_decades AS (
  SELECT 
    book_id,
    (CAST(pub_year AS INTEGER) / 10) * 10 as decade,
    rating_number
  FROM book_years
  WHERE pub_year IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT 
  decade,
  COUNT(DISTINCT bd.book_id) as num_books,
  AVG(CAST(r.rating AS FLOAT)) as avg_rating
FROM book_decades bd
JOIN review_database.review r 
  ON r.purchase_id = 'purchaseid_' || SUBSTRING(bd.book_id FROM 'bookid_(.+)$')
GROUP BY decade
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT bd.book_id) >= 10
ORDER BY avg_rating DESC
LIMIT 10;

WITH book_years AS (
  SELECT 
    book_id,
    CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '(19[0-9]{2}|20[0-2][0-3])') AS INTEGER) as pub_year,
    rating_number
  FROM books_info
  WHERE details ~ '(19[0-9]{2}|20[0-2][0-3])'
),
book_decades AS (
  SELECT 
    book_id,
    (CAST(pub_year AS INTEGER) / 10) * 10 as decade,
    rating_number
  FROM book_years
  WHERE pub_year IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT 
  decade,
  COUNT(DISTINCT bd.book_id) as num_books,
  AVG(CAST(r.rating AS FLOAT)) as avg_rating
FROM book_decades bd
JOIN review_database.review r 
  ON r.purchase_id = 'purchaseid_' || SUBSTRING(bd.book_id FROM 'bookid_(.+)$')
GROUP BY decade
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT bd.book_id) >= 10
ORDER BY avg_rating DESC
LIMIT 10;

args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Load books data from file
books_file = var_functions.query_db:24
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

print(f"Total books: {len(books_data)}")

# Function to extract publication year from details
def extract_pub_year(details_text):
    if not details_text:
        return None
    
    # Find 4-digit years between 1900-2023
    years = re.findall(r'(\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d|2023)\b)', str(details_text))
    if years:
        year_ints = [int(year[0]) for year in years]
        # Return the smallest year (most likely the original publication year)
        return min(year_ints)
    return None

# Extract publication data from books
books_processed = []
for book in books_data:

            'rating_count': rating_count
        })

# Show some matching patterns
for review in review_data[:10]:

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Load books data from file
books_file = var_functions.query_db:24
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

print(f"Total books: {len(books_data)}")

# Function to extract publication year from details
def extract_pub_year(details_text):
    if not details_text:
        return None
    
    # Find 4-digit years between 1900-2023
    years = re.findall(r'(\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d|2023)\b)', str(details_text))
    if years:
        year_ints = [int(year[0]) for year in years]
        # Return the smallest year (most likely the original publication year)
        return min(year_ints)
    return None

# Extract publication data from books
books_processed = []
for book in books_data:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    pub_year = extract_pub_year(details)
    
    if pub_year:
        decade = (pub_year // 10) * 10
        rating_number = book.get('rating_number', '0')
        try:
            rating_count = int(rating_number) if rating_number else 0
        except:
            rating_count = 0
            
        books_processed.append({
            'book_id': book_id,
            'pub_year': pub_year,
            'decade': decade,
            'rating_count': rating_count
        })

print(f"Books with publication year: {len(books_processed)}")
print(f"Decade range: {min([b['decade'] for b in processed_books])} to {max([b['decade'] for b in processed_books])}")

# Display some examples
for i, book in enumerate(processed_books[:10]):
    print(f"{i+1}. {book['book_id']} - Year: {book['pub_year']}, Decade: {book['decade']}, Ratings: {book['rating_count']}")

# Now load review data
review_file = var_functions.query_db:22
with open(review_file, 'r') as f:
    review_data = json.load(f)

print(f"\nTotal reviews: {len(review_data)}")

# Look at purchase_id patterns in reviews
purchase_ids = [review.get('purchase_id') for review in review_data[:20]]
print("Sample purchase_ids:", purchase_ids)

# Check if any reviews have purchase_id that matches book_id format
bookid_reviews = [r for r in review_data if str(r.get('purchase_id', '')).startswith('bookid_')]
print(f"Reviews with bookid_ format: {len(bookid_reviews)}")

# Show some matching patterns
for review in review_data[:10]:
    print(f"Review purchase_id: {review.get('purchase_id')}, Rating: {review.get('rating')}")

result = {
    'books': books_processed,
    'total_reviews': len(review_data),
    'bookid_format_reviews': len(bookid_reviews)
}

print("\n__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))

args.code:
import json
import re
import os

# Load books data
with open(var_functions.query_db:24, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

print(f'Total books: {len(books_data)}')

# Extract publication years and decades
books_by_decade = {}
valid_books = 0

for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    book_id = book['book_id']
    
    # Extract year (1900-2023)
    match = re.search(r'(\b(19[0-9]{2}|20[0-2][0-9]|2023)\b)', details)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        decade = (year // 10) * 10
        valid_books += 1
        
        if decade not in books_by_decade:
            books_by_decade[decade] = []
        
        books_by_decade[decade].append(book_id)

print(f'Books with valid year: {valid_books}')
print(f'Decades: {sorted(books_by_decade.keys())}')

# Check review data
with open(var_functions.query_db:22, 'r') as f:
    review_data = json.load(f)

print(f'Total reviews: {len(review_data)}')

# Map review purchase_id to book_id
review_purchases = [r['purchase_id'] for r in review_data]
book_ids = [b['book_id'] for b in books_data]

print(f'Sample review purchase_ids: {review_purchases[:20]}')
print(f'Sample book_ids: {book_ids[:20]}')

# See if any pattern matches
matching = 0
for review in review_data[:20]:
    rev_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = review['rating']
    
    # Check if it could be derived from book_id
    if rev_id.startswith('purchase'):
        # Try to extract number and match to book_id number
        num_match = re.search(r'(\d+)', rev_id)
        if num_match:
            num = num_match.group(1)
            expected_book_id = f'bookid_{num}'
            if expected_book_id in book_ids:
                matching += 1

print(f'Found {matching} potential matches')

result = {'books_by_decade': books_by_decade, 'total_reviews': len(review_data)}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re
import os

# Load books data
with open(var_functions.query_db:24, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

print(f'Total books: {len(books_data)}')

# Extract publication years and decades
books_by_decade = {}
valid_books = 0

for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    book_id = book['book_id']
    
    # Extract year (1900-2023)
    match = re.search(r'(\b(19[0-9]{2}|20[0-2][0-9]|2023)\b)', details)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        decade = (year // 10) * 10
        valid_books += 1
        
        if decade not in books_by_decade:
            books_by_decade[decade] = []
        
        books_by_decade[decade].append(book_id)

print(f'Books with valid year: {valid_books}')
print(f'Decades: {sorted(books_by_decade.keys())}')

# Check review data
with open(var_functions.query_db:22, 'r') as f:
    review_data = json.load(f)

print(f'Total reviews: {len(review_data)}')

# Map review purchase_id to book_id
review_purchases = [r['purchase_id'] for r in review_data]
book_ids = [b['book_id'] for b in books_data]

print(f'Sample review purchase_ids: {review_purchases[:20]}')
print(f'Sample book_ids: {book_ids[:20]}')

# See if any pattern matches
matching = 0
for review in review_data[:20]:
    rev_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = review['rating']
    
    # Check if it could be derived from book_id
    if rev_id.startswith('purchase'):
        # Try to extract number and match to book_id number
        num_match = re.search(r'(\d+)', rev_id)
        if num_match:
            num = num_match.group(1)
            expected_book_id = f'bookid_{num}'
            if expected_book_id in book_ids:
                matching += 1

print(f'Found {matching} potential matches')

result = {'books_by_decade': books_by_decade, 'total_reviews': len(review_data)}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re

# Load the file path from storage
books_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']
print(f"Books file path: {books_file_path}")

# Read books data
with open(books_file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

print(f"Total books loaded: {len(books_data)}")

# Extract publication years from details
def get_pub_year(details):
    if not details:
        return None
    # Find years 1900-2023
    matches = re.findall(r'\b(19[0-9]{2}|20[0-2][0-9]|2023)\b', str(details))
    if matches:
        years = [int(y) for y in matches]
        return min(years)  # Earliest year is likely publication year
    return None

# Process books to extract decades
books_with_years = []
for book in books_data:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    pub_year = get_pub_year(details)
    
    if pub_year:
        decade = (pub_year // 10) * 10
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book_id,
            'pub_year': pub_year,
            'decade': decade
        })

print(f"Books with publication years: {len(books_with_years)}")
print(f"Year range: {min([b['pub_year'] for b in books_with_years])} to {max([b['pub_year'] for b in books_with_years])}")

# Show decade distribution
from collections import Counter
decade_counts = Counter([b['decade'] for b in books_with_years])
print(f"Books per decade: {sorted(decade_counts.items())}")

result = {
    'books_with_years': books_with_years,
    'decade_counts': dict(decade_counts)
}
print("\n__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import pandas as pd
import json
import re

# Load books and review data
books_file = '/tmp/tmp2qdy2u34.json'
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

print(f'Loaded {len(books_data)} books')

# Extract publication year from details using regex
for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    # Find all years 1900-2023
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19[0-9]{2}|20[0-2][0-9]|2023)\b', details)
    if years:
        # Use the earliest year (original publication)
        pub_year = min([int(y) for y in years])
        book['pub_year'] = pub_year
        book['decade'] = (pub_year // 10) * 10
    else:
        book['pub_year'] = None
        book['decade'] = None

# Filter books with publication year
books_df = pd.DataFrame([b for b in books_data if b['pub_year'] is not None])
print(f'Books with publication year: {len(books_df)}')
print(f'Decade range: {books_df["decade"].min()} to {books_df["decade"].max()}')

# Load review data
review_file = '/tmp/tmp1g5c2xqw.json'
with open(review_file, 'r') as f:
    review_data = json.load(f)

reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(review_data)
print(f'Loaded {len(reviews_df)} reviews')

# Show purchase_id patterns
print('Sample purchase_ids:', reviews_df['purchase_id'].head(10).tolist())
print('Sample book_ids:', books_df['book_id'].head(10).tolist())

# Try to find mapping - extract numbers
reviews_df['purchase_num'] = reviews_df['purchase_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)
books_df['book_num'] = books_df['book_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)

print('Unique purchase nums range:', reviews_df['purchase_num'].min(), 'to', reviews_df['purchase_num'].max())
print('Unique book nums range:', books_df['book_num'].min(), 'to', books_df['book_num'].max())

result = {
    'books_count': len(books_df),
    'reviews_count': len(reviews_df)
}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.query:
WITH extracted_years AS (
  SELECT 
    book_id,
    (regexp_match(details, '(\d{4})'))[1]::int as pub_year
  FROM books_info
  WHERE details ~ '(\d{4})'
),
valid_books AS (
  SELECT 
    book_id,
    pub_year,
    (pub_year / 10) * 10 as decade
  FROM extracted_years
  WHERE pub_year BETWEEN 1900 AND 2023
)
SELECT 
  book_id,
  decade
FROM valid_books
ORDER BY book_id;

args.code:
import json
import re
import sqlite3

# Load the data from stored files
books_file = 'var_functions.query_db:24'
review_file = 'var_functions.query_db:36'

# Get books data - it's already returned as JSON
books_data = var_functions.query_db:24

# Get review data - it's a file path
with open(var_functions.query_db:36, 'r') as f:
    review_data = json.load(f)

print(f'Books: {len(books_data)}')
print(f'Reviews: {len(review_data)}')

# Show the pattern - book_ids and purchase_ids
print(f'Sample book_ids: {[b["book_id"] for b in books_data[:10]]}')
print(f'Sample purchase_ids: {[r["purchase_id"] for r in review_data[:10]]}')

# Create mapping: purchaseid_XXX -> bookid_XXX
bookid_to_decade = {}
for book in books_data:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    decade = book['decade']
    # Extract the number from bookid_XXX
    num_match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book_id)
    if num_match:
        num = num_match.group(1)
        bookid_to_decade[f'purchaseid_{num}'] = decade

print(f'Mapped {len(bookid_to_decade)} books to purchase format')

# Now process reviews and calculate average ratings by decade
decade_stats = {}
review_count = 0

for review in review_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    # Map purchase_id to decade if possible
    if purchase_id in bookid_to_decade:
        decade = bookid_to_decade[purchase_id]
        review_count += 1
        
        if decade not in decade_stats:
            decade_stats[decade] = {'total_rating': 0, 'count': 0, 'book_ids': set()}
        
        decade_stats[decade]['total_rating'] += rating
        decade_stats[decade]['count'] += 1
        decade_stats[decade]['book_ids'].add(purchase_id)

# Calculate averages and filter decades with at least 10 books
results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(stats['book_ids'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / stats['count']
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_reviews': stats['count']
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print(f'Reviews matched to books: {review_count}')
print('Decades with >= 10 books (sorted by avg rating):')
for r in results:
    print(f"{r['decade']}s: avg_rating={r['avg_rating']}, books={r['num_books']}, reviews={r['num_reviews']}")

# Show top result
if results:
    print(f"\nTop decade: {results[0]['decade']}s")

output = {'results': results, 'total_mapped_reviews': review_count}
print('\n__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:6.text: Good reference guide for the basics

val_args.code:
import json
import re

# Access stored data directly
books_data = var_functions.query_db:42
review_data_file = var_functions.query_db:36

# Load review data from file
with open(review_data_file, 'r') as f:
    review_data = json.load(f)

print(f'Books with decades: {len(books_data)}')
print(f'Total reviews: {len(review_data)}')

# Create dictionary: book_number -> decade
book_num_to_decade = {}
for book in books_data:
    num_match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
    if num_match:
        num = num_match.group(1)
        book_num_to_decade[num] = int(book['decade'])

print(f'Books mapped: {len(book_num_to_decade)}')

# Process reviews
decade_stats = {}
matched_reviews = 0

for review in review_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    # Extract number from purchaseid_XXX
    num_match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', purchase_id)
    if num_match:
        num = num_match.group(1)
        if num in book_num_to_decade:
            decade = book_num_to_decade[num]
            matched_reviews += 1
            
            if decade not in decade_stats:
                decade_stats[decade] = {'total_rating': 0, 'review_count': 0, 'books': set()}
            
            decade_stats[decade]['total_rating'] += rating
            decade_stats[decade]['review_count'] += 1
            decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(purchase_id)

print(f'Matched reviews: {matched_reviews}')

# Calculate averages for decades with >= 10 books
results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(stats['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / stats['review_count']
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_reviews': stats['review_count']
        })

results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('Top decades:')
for r in results[:5]:
    print(f"{r['decade']}s: avg={r['avg_rating']} ({r['num_books']} books, {r['num_reviews']} reviews)")

output = {'results': results}
print('\n__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))
