args.code:
import json
import re

# Load books data (decades already extracted)
books_data = var_functions.query_db:42

# Load review data
review_file = var_functions.query_db:36
with open(review_file, 'r') as f:
    review_data = json.load(f)

print('Books with decade info: ' + str(len(books_data)))
print('Total reviews: ' + str(len(review_data)))

# Create mapping from book number to decade
decade_map = {}
for book in books_data:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    decade = book['decade']
    num = book_id.replace('bookid_', '')
    decade_map[num] = int(decade)

print('Mapped ' + str(len(decade_map)) + ' books to decades')

# Process reviews by decade
decade_reviews = {}
matched_count = 0

for review in review_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    try:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        num = purchase_id.replace('purchaseid_', '')
        
        if num in decade_map:
            decade = decade_map[num]
            matched_count += 1
            
            if decade not in decade_reviews:
                decade_reviews[decade] = {'ratings': [], 'books': set()}
            
            decade_reviews[decade]['ratings'].append(rating)
            decade_reviews[decade]['books'].add(purchase_id)
    except:
        continue

print('Matched ' + str(matched_count) + ' reviews')

# Calculate average ratings for decades with >= 10 books
results = []
for decade, data in decade_reviews.items():
    num_books = len(data['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_reviews': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('Found ' + str(len(results)) + ' decades with >= 10 books')
print('Top results:')
for i, r in enumerate(results[:10]):
    print(str(r['decade']) + 's: avg=' + str(r['avg_rating']) + ', books=' + str(r['num_books']))

output = {
    'top_decade': results[0] if results else None,
    'all_decades': results
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))

    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    try:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        num = purchase_id.replace('purchaseid_', '')
        
        if num in decade_map:
            decade = decade_map[num]
            matched_count += 1
            
            if decade not in decade_reviews:
                decade_reviews[decade] = {'ratings': [], 'books': set()}
            
            decade_reviews[decade]['ratings'].append(rating)
            decade_reviews[decade]['books'].add(purchase_id)
    except:
        continue

print('Matched ' + str(matched_count) + ' reviews')

# Calculate average ratings for decades with >= 10 books
results = []
for decade, data in decade_reviews.items():
    num_books = len(data['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_reviews': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('Found ' + str(len(results)) + ' decades with >= 10 books')
print('Top results:')
for i, r in enumerate(results[:10]):
    print(str(r['decade']) + 's: avg=' + str(r['avg_rating']) + ', books=' + str(r['num_books']))

output = {
    'top_decade': results[0] if results else None,
    'all_decades': results
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re

# Get data from storage
books_with_decades = var_functions.query_db:42
review_file_path = var_functions.query_db:36

with open(review_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Books:', len(books_with_decades))
print('Reviews:', len(reviews))

# Map book numbers to decades
book_map = {}
for book in books_with_decades:
    num = book['book_id'].split('_')[1]
    book_map[num] = int(book['decade'])

# Process reviews
stats = {}
matched = 0

for r in reviews:
    try:
        num = r['purchase_id'].split('_')[1]
        if num in book_map:
            decade = book_map[num]
            rating = float(r['rating'])
            matched += 1
            
            if decade not in stats:
                stats[decade] = {'total': 0, 'count': 0, 'books': set()}
            
            stats[decade]['total'] += rating
            stats[decade]['count'] += 1
            stats[decade]['books'].add(num)
    except:
        pass

print('Matched:', matched)

# Calculate results
results = []
for decade, data in stats.items():
    books = len(data['books'])
    if books >= 10:
        avg = data['total'] / data['count']
        results.append([decade, round(avg, 3), books, data['count']])

results.sort(key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
print('Results:', results[:5])

output = {'top': results[0] if results else None, 'all': results}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re

# Get books data (list of dicts with book_id and decade)
books_data = var_functions.query_db:42

# Get reviews data (file path)
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db:36
with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

print('Loaded ' + str(len(books_data)) + ' books with decades')
print('Loaded ' + str(len(reviews_data)) + ' reviews')

# Create mapping: book_number -> decade
book_num_to_decade = {}
for book in books_data:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    decade = int(book['decade'])
    # Extract number from bookid_X
    match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book_id)
    if match:
        num = match.group(1)
        book_num_to_decade[num] = decade

print('Mapped ' + str(len(book_num_to_decade)) + ' books')

# Process reviews: calculate average rating by decade
# Only include decades with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = {}
matched_reviews = 0

for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    # Extract number from purchaseid_X
    match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', purchase_id)
    if match:
        num = match.group(1)
        if num in book_num_to_decade:
            decade = book_num_to_decade[num]
            matched_reviews += 1
            
            if decade not in decade_stats:
                decade_stats[decade] = {'total_rating': 0, 'review_count': 0, 'book_ids': set()}
            
            decade_stats[decade]['total_rating'] += rating
            decade_stats[decade]['review_count'] += 1
            decade_stats[decade]['book_ids'].add(purchase_id)

print('Matched ' + str(matched_reviews) + ' reviews to books')

# Calculate average rating for each decade with >= 10 distinct books
results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(stats['book_ids'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / stats['review_count']
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_reviews': stats['review_count']
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('Found ' + str(len(results)) + ' decades with >= 10 books')
for r in results[:5]:
    print(str(r['decade']) + 's: avg=' + str(r['avg_rating']) + ' (books=' + str(r['num_books']) + ', reviews=' + str(r['num_reviews']) + ')')

# Get top decade
top_decade = results[0] if results else None

output = {'top_decade': top_decade, 'all_decades': results}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

import json
import re

# Load books data (file path stored in var)
books_file_key = 'var_functions.query_db:42'
books_file = locals()[books_file_key]
print('Books file path: ' + str(books_file))

# For now, extract the data from previous query result which is already in JSON format
# Extracting from the preview - use first few rows as sample
books_text = '''[
{"book_id": "bookid_1", "decade": "2000"},
{"book_id": "bookid_10", "decade": "2000"},
{"book_id": "bookid_102", "decade": "1920"},
{"book_id": "bookid_105", "decade": "1980"},
{"book_id": "bookid_11", "decade": "1990"},
{"book_id": "bookid_12", "decade": "2020"},
{"book_id": "bookid_2", "decade": "1990"},
{"book_id": "bookid_21", "decade": "1940"},
{"book_id": "bookid_24", "decade": "1930"},
{"book_id": "bookid_3", "decade": "2010"}
]'''

books_data = json.loads(books_text)
print('Sample books: ' + str(len(books_data)))

# Load review data
review_file_key = 'var_functions.query_db:36'
review_file = locals()[review_file_key]
with open(review_file, 'r') as f:
    review_data = json.load(f)
    
print('Reviews: ' + str(len(review_data)))
print('Sample purchase_ids: ' + str([r['purchase_id'] for r in review_data[:5]]))

# Create quick mapping (demo with sample)
book_map = {}
for b in books_data:
    num = b['book_id'].split('_')[1]
    book_map[num] = int(b['decade'])

print('Map: ' + str(book_map))
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({'status': 'loaded'}))

Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

{"book_id": "bookid_1", "decade": "2000"},
{"book_id": "bookid_10", "decade": "2000"},

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

args.query:
SELECT 
  decade,
  COUNT(DISTINCT book_id) as num_books
FROM (
  SELECT 
    book_id,
    (CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '(\d{4})') AS INTEGER) / 10) * 10 as decade
  FROM books_info
  WHERE details ~ '\d{4}'
) AS book_decades
GROUP BY decade
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT book_id) >= 10
ORDER BY decade;

args.code:
import json
import re
import os

# Load books with decades data (stored as JSON list)
books_with_decades = var_functions.query_db:42

# Load review data (stored as file path)
review_data_file = var_functions.query_db:36

# Check if review_data_file is a file path
if isinstance(review_data_file, str) and os.path.exists(review_data_file):
    with open(review_data_file, 'r') as f:
        review_data = json.load(f)
else:
    # Data is already in JSON format
    review_data = review_data_file

print('Loaded ' + str(len(books_with_decades)) + ' books with decades')
print('Loaded ' + str(len(review_data)) + ' reviews')

# Map book numbers to decades
book_num_to_decade = {}
for book in books_with_decades:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    decade = int(book['decade'])
    # Extract number from bookid_X
    match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book_id)
    if match:
        num = match.group(1)
        book_num_to_decade[num] = decade

print('Mapped ' + str(len(book_num_to_decade)) + ' books')

# Process reviews and calculate average ratings by decade
# Only include decades with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = {}
matched_reviews = 0

for review in review_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    try:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        
        # Extract number from purchaseid_X
        match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', purchase_id)
        if match:
            num = match.group(1)
            if num in book_num_to_decade:
                decade = book_num_to_decade[num]
                matched_reviews += 1
                
                if decade not in decade_stats:
                    decade_stats[decade] = {'total_rating': 0, 'review_count': 0, 'book_ids': set()}
                
                decade_stats[decade]['total_rating'] += rating
                decade_stats[decade]['review_count'] += 1
                decade_stats[decade]['book_ids'].add(purchase_id)
    except:
        continue

print('Matched ' + str(matched_reviews) + ' reviews')

# Calculate average rating for each decade with >= 10 distinct books
results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(stats['book_ids'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / stats['review_count']
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_reviews': stats['review_count']
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('Found ' + str(len(results)) + ' decades with >= 10 books')
for r in results:
    print(str(r['decade']) + 's: avg=' + str(r['avg_rating']) + ' (books=' + str(r['num_books']) + ', reviews=' + str(r['num_reviews']) + ')')

# Get top result
top_result = results[0] if results else None

final_output = {
    'top_decade': top_result,
    'all_decades': results
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(final_output))

    with open(review_data_file, 'r') as f:
        review_data = json.load(f)
else:
    # Data is already in JSON format
    review_data = review_data_file

print('Loaded ' + str(len(books_with_decades)) + ' books with decades')
print('Loaded ' + str(len(review_data)) + ' reviews')

# Map book numbers to decades
book_num_to_decade = {}
for book in books_with_decades:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    decade = int(book['decade'])
    # Extract number from bookid_X
    match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book_id)
    if match:
        num = match.group(1)
        book_num_to_decade[num] = decade

print('Mapped ' + str(len(book_num_to_decade)) + ' books')

# Process reviews and calculate average ratings by decade
# Only include decades with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = {}
matched_reviews = 0

for review in review_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    try:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        
        # Extract number from purchaseid_X
        match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', purchase_id)
        if match:
            num = match.group(1)
            if num in book_num_to_decade:
                decade = book_num_to_decade[num]
                matched_reviews += 1
                
                if decade not in decade_stats:
                    decade_stats[decade] = {'total_rating': 0, 'review_count': 0, 'book_ids': set()}
                
                decade_stats[decade]['total_rating'] += rating
                decade_stats[decade]['review_count'] += 1
                decade_stats[decade]['book_ids'].add(purchase_id)
    except:
        continue

print('Matched ' + str(matched_reviews) + ' reviews')

# Calculate average rating for each decade with >= 10 distinct books
results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(stats['book_ids'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / stats['review_count']
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_reviews': stats['review_count']
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('Found ' + str(len(results)) + ' decades with >= 10 books')
for r in results:
    print(str(r['decade']) + 's: avg=' + str(r['avg_rating']) + ' (books=' + str(r['num_books']) + ', reviews=' + str(r['num_reviews']) + ')')

# Get top result
top_result = results[0] if results else None

final_output = {
    'top_decade': top_result,
    'all_decades': results
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(final_output))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

import json
import re

# Load the data
books_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:42']
if isinstance(books_file_path, str):
    with open(books_file_path, 'r') as f:
        books_with_decades = json.load(f)
else:
    books_with_decades = books_file_path

review_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
with open(review_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print(f'Books with decades: {len(books_with_decades)}')
print(f'Reviews: {len(reviews)}')

# Create mapping from book number to decade
book_map = {}
for book in books_with_decades:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    decade = book['decade']
    num = book_id.replace('bookid_', '')
    book_map[num] = int(decade)

print(f'Mapped {len(book_map)} books')

# Calculate average ratings by decade
# Only include decades with >= 10 distinct books
decade_data = {}
matched_count = 0

for review in reviews:
    # Get purchase_id
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    
    try:
        # Extract number from purchaseid_X
        num = purchase_id.replace('purchaseid_', '')
        
        # Check if we have a decade for this book
        if num in book_map:
            decade = book_map[num]
            rating = float(review['rating'])
            
            matched_count += 1
            
            if decade not in decade_data:
                decade_data[decade] = {'ratings': [], 'books': set()}
            
            decade_data[decade]['ratings'].append(rating)
            decade_data[decade]['books'].add(num)
    except:
        continue

# Calculate averages for decades with >= 10 books
results = []
for decade, data in decade_data.items():
    num_books = len(data['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_reviews': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

# Top result
top_decade = results[0] if results else None

print(f'Matched reviews: {matched_count}')
print(f'Decades with >= 10 books: {len(results)}')
if top_decade:
    print(f'Top: {top_decade["decade"]}s with avg rating {top_decade["avg_rating"]}')

# Format final answer
final_answer = {
    'answer': f"{top_decade['decade']}s" if top_decade else "No data available",
    'top_decade': top_decade,
    'all_results': results
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(final_answer))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!
